title: SEGMENT 4 — SMRT cell architecture: millions of wells, one polymerase per well

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — SMRT cell architecture: millions of wells, one polymerase per well
Alright.
So we have the physical idea — the zero-mode waveguide, this exquisitely small illuminated volume at the bottom of a sub-wavelength hole.
Now let's zoom out and look at how PacBio assembles millions of these wells into a sequencing chip, the SMRT cell.
SMRT stands for Single Molecule Real Time.
The acronym is the company's whole pitch, compressed into four letters.
A SMRT cell is the consumable chip you load into a PacBio instrument to run a single sequencing experiment.
Physically, it looks like a small rectangular slide, maybe the size of a microscope cover slip.
Inside, it's an extraordinary piece of nanofabrication.
Picture it this way.

A SMRT cell is a metal plate with millions of microscopic holes — the zero-mode waveguides — punched through it, mounted on a transparent glass substrate, with a flow chamber on top to deliver the sequencing reagents.
The metal is aluminum.
The plate is about a hundred nanometers thick.
The holes are about seventy nanometers across.
And there are, in the modern chips, eight million or more of them, arranged in a precise grid.
At the bottom of each hole sits — ideally — one DNA polymerase enzyme, anchored to the glass.
Loaded onto that polymerase is one circular DNA template — the SMRTbell template, which we'll meet in segment seven.
Above the polymerase, filling the flow chamber, is a solution containing all four nucleotide types, each one labeled with a different colored fluorescent dye.
The instrument shines laser light up through the glass, into the bottom of every well, simultaneously.
And the instrument's camera watches the array, recording the color and timing of fluorescent pulses from every well in parallel.
That parallelism is what makes the throughput possible.
Each well is producing one read from one polymerase.
By itself, that's slow — a single polymerase reads only a few bases per second, so even running for hours, one well only generates tens of thousands of bases.
But you have millions of wells running simultaneously.
Eight million wells times some tens of kilobases per well equals on the order of hundreds of gigabases of sequence per cell.
That's the magic of massive parallelism, the same trick Illumina uses, but executed on a totally different substrate.
Now here's a subtle but important engineering problem: how do you get one polymerase into each well?
Not zero.
Not two.
Exactly one.
The wells are loaded by simply incubating the chip in a solution of polymerase-template complexes — the polymerases already bound to their templates — and letting them diffuse into the wells.
They get caught at the bottom by the streptavidin anchor.
But the loading is statistical.
If you use a low concentration of complexes, most wells get zero, a few get one, almost none get two.
If you use a high concentration, most wells get two or more, which is unusable because you can't tell which polymerase's signal is which.
This is Poisson loading.

The math says that the maximum fraction of wells that can have exactly one polymerase, with random loading, is about thirty-seven percent — when the average loading is exactly one per well, the Poisson distribution gives about 37% empty, 37% single, and 26% with two or more.
So under random loading, you can never use more than about a third of your wells productively.
The rest are either empty (no signal) or multiply loaded (uninterpretable signal).
PacBio has worked very hard to push past this limit using what they call magnetic bead loading and other tricks that bias the loading away from purely random.
With these methods, they can achieve much higher single-occupancy rates — sixty, seventy, even eighty percent of wells getting exactly one polymerase.
That's enormous for throughput, because it directly multiplies the usable fraction of the chip.
A few more architectural notes.
The flow cell on top of the chip allows continuous delivery of fresh reagents during the sequencing run.
The reactions take place at body temperature, roughly thirty degrees Celsius, in an aqueous buffer that mimics cellular conditions.
The reactions can run for hours — a single SMRT cell run, on a modern instrument, takes about thirty hours.
The optics underneath are complex.
Multiple laser lines, one for each dye color.
A field-of-view large enough to see every well at once, focused through high-numerical-aperture objectives.
High-speed cameras, capturing at hundreds of frames per second, generating terabytes of raw image data per run.
The instrument also has heating, fluidics, and stabilization to keep the optical alignment constant over the long run.
And the chip is a single-use consumable.
Once you've run it for thirty hours and harvested your data, you throw it away.
The chip itself, with all its nanofabrication, is the bulk of the cost-per-run.

Driving down the cost of the chip — which they do by making chips with more wells, so cost per gigabase drops even as cost per chip stays similar or grows — has been a major focus of PacBio's engineering for the past decade.
The lineage of SMRT cells over time tracks the throughput growth of the platform.
The original RS chips had around 75,000 wells per cell.
The Sequel chips, launched in 2015, jumped to a million wells.
The Sequel II chips, in 2019, jumped to eight million.
The Revio chips, the current generation as of the early 2020s, are at twenty-five million per cell, and PacBio runs four of them simultaneously per instrument.
Each generation has roughly an order of magnitude more throughput than the last.
We'll cover the instrument lineage in detail in segment ten.

The conceptual point I want to leave you with here is: the SMRT cell is a chip of independent single-molecule reactions, observed in parallel, made possible by integrating semiconductor-style nanofabrication with biological chemistry.
It's a piece of physics, a piece of chemistry, and a piece of microfabrication, all stacked into a single consumable.
The marriage of those three disciplines is what PacBio is, at its core.
Now let's look at the chemistry of the nucleotides themselves, because that's the next piece of the puzzle.
